DISCOVERY 302, DEFENDANT ANN CAZAHOUS' MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES BY CROSS-DEFENDANT CHRISTOPHER COLE TO CROSS-COMPLAINANT'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS SETS ONE AND TWO IS GRANTED AND DENIED, AS FOLLOWS. THE RFPS FALL INTO THREE CATEGORIES, GROUP 1 (1-12), GROUP 2 (20, 21, 23, 27-28), AND GROUP 3 (40-42, 46-49, 52). AS TO ALL GROUPS, CROSS-DEFENDANT COLE MAKES NEW ADDITIONAL OBJECTIONS IN AMENDED RESPONSES THAT WERE NOT CONSIDERED. A PARTY MAY NOT AMEND THEIR RESPONSES TO ADD NEW OBJECTIONS. AS TO GROUPS 1 AND 3, THE OBJECTION THAT THE REQUESTS FAILS TO SPECIFY THE DOCUMENTS WITH REASONABLE PARTICULARITY IS SUSTAINED. A REQUEST FOR ADMISSION OR AN AVERMENT IN AN ARBITRATION BRIEF IS NOT A CONTENTION OF A PARTY. HOWEVER, IN COLE'S ORIGINAL RESPONSES, HE AGREED TO PRODUCE DOCUMENTS. THEREFORE, THE COURT ORDERS THESE DOCUMENTS TO BE PRODUCED. AS TO GROUP 2, COLE IS ORDERED TO AMEND HIS RESPONSES TO STATE THE REASON THAT THE DOCUMENTS ARE NOT BEING PRODUCED (EITHER THEY NEVER EXISTED OR WERE DESTROYED). AMENDED, VERIFIED RESPONSES SHALL BE SERVED WITHIN ONE WEEK OF NOTICE OF THIS ORDER. IF AMENDED AND VERIFIED RESPONSES ARE NOT SERVED WITHIN ONE WEEK OF THIS ORDER, SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $2500 SHALL BE DUE AND PAYABLE BY COLE IN FAVOR OF CAZAHOUS WITHIN TWO WEEKS OF NOTICE OF THIS ORDER. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE PRO TEM PETER CATALANOTTI, CLERK K. LIU, NOT REPORTED. (D302/JPT)

LAW AND MOTION 302, CROSS DEFENDANT CHRISTOPHER COLE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS GRANTED. SUPERIOR COURT (2013) 107 CAL.APP.4TH 1052, 1060. THERE, THE COURT EXPLAINS THAT WHERE THE FEES EXCEED THE VALUE OF THE SERVICES RENDERED, THE CLIENT INCURS CONTRACT DAMAGES. MS. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Third Amended Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Jan-25-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Damon Thurston, Esq. 510-465-2233 of Rankin Sproat Mires Reynolds Shuey & Mintz for Plaintiff/Cross-Defendant Christoper Cole.
Mark Edson, Esq. 510-444-7688 of Bennett Samuelsen Reynolds Allard Cowperthwaite & Gelini for Cross-Complainant William Balin, Esq. 925-89-4403 who is also present.
Robert Gonzales, Esq. 415-238-0749 for Defendant Ann Cazahous.

RULING - The matter is ARGUED. Cross-Defendant withdraws request to continue trial. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes)The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS COMPLAINANT ANN CAZAHOUS' Motion For Leave To File Third Amended Cross-Complaint.

Cross-complainant Ann Cazahous' motion for leave to file a third amended cross-complaint is granted. Ms. Cazahous is granted leave to file her proposed third amended cross-complaint by January 26, 2018 and cross-defendants' answers to the second amended cross-complaint will be deemed to apply to the third amended cross-complaint. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing leave to amend pleadings at any time during the pendency of a case. There is no or very minimal prejudice to cross-defendants by granting the motion since the same factual/legal issue underlying the proposed new cross-claim also underlies one or more Ms. Cazahous' defenses to the claims asserted against her, notwithstanding the arguable lack of diligence by Ms. Cazahous in filing this motion. Nor have cross-defendants shown that Mr. Cazahous' proposed new cross-claim is indistinguishable from her prior claim for legal malpractice which was adjudicated against her. The new claim does not seek malpractice type damages, but rather is in the nature of a refund action for monies paid, which, as Judge Cheng suggested, is not a cognizable malpractice claim, but may be a viable contract claim. Cross-Defendant has not requested a trial continuance. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, mtorreano@sftc.org, Reported. (D302/HK)

RULING - The matter is ARGUED. Cross-Defendant withdraws request to continue trial. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes)Defendant argues deposition date and documents. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT WILLIAM BALIN, BALIN & KOTLER, LLP's Motion To Compel Defendant/Cross-Complainant/Cross-Defendant Ann Cazahous To Appear For Deposition, Produce Documents And Pay Monitary Sanctions.

Cross-complainants William Balin and Balin and Cotter's motion to compel the deposition of cross-defendant Ann Cazahous, to require her to produce documents and for monetary sanctions is granted in its entirety. Ms. Cazhous must make herself available for four and a half hours of deposition no later than January 30, 2018 and must produce all documents requested in the notice of deposition other than privileged documents. Ms. Cazahous must also pay $1,600.50 in sanctions to Mr. Balin and his firm no later than February 28, 2018. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Dec-27-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Mark Edson, Esq. 510-444-7688 of Bennett Samuelsen Reynolds Allard Cowperthwaite & Gelini for Cross Defendant William Balin, Balin & Kotler LLP.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. An email was received prior to the hearing that the contest was withdrawn. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT CHRISTOPHER COLE's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication.

Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Christopher Cole's motion for summary judgment is granted. The gravamen of the malpractice claim is that Ms. Cazahous paid $52,000 for legal services that were worthless/unnecessary. The claim fails under Orrick Herrington & Sutcliffe v. Superior Court (2013) 107 Cal.App.4th 1052, 1060. There, the court explains that where the fees exceed the value of the services rendered, the client incurs contract damages. Ms. Cazahous's remedy is to sue for breach of contract rather than for malpractice. The tort claim for legal malpractice fails as a matter of law based on the damages that Ms. Cazahous seeks to recover.
